Thanks for this. Unfortunately, I don't think markdown is yet supported on PyPI (at least not in the long_description). If I were to accept this pull, on the next release, the [package page](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/irc) would not render properly.

I know there is some support for markdown in PyPI if no long description is provided, so removing the long_description may be a solution, except that in Python projects, I also tend to use restructured text for the CHANGES file and the documentation, often pulling the README or CHANGES into the documentation.

I think a better solution might be to rename the file to README.rst, which I believe Github will recognize as reStructuredText and render nicely, and also do the same with the CHANGES file.

If by replacing the old thumbnail you mean to physically replace the existing file, that is something you can't do. Doing so will break the unit of work and transaction support in DEPOT.

DEPOT is only able to rollback file changes when the transaction is rolled back exactly because it never replaces anything and only creates new entities. So when you rollback the transaction you just delete the new file and keep the old one, while when committing the transaction you keep the new one and delete the old old.

def func():
  if  blockfunc():
       asyncfunc()
       blockfunc()
       blockfunc()

I just thought hat I can just wrap func() with @async_thread and it will work. Because running function in another thread is like async function at some level of abstraction. And it will be Ok that those async parts are no more async. Let them be blocking (if its possible). Because in context of main thread where Kernel is run they are really still async.

That is my thought how I see this.

See the flag "272", in particular the "256" bit. This is the so-called "secondary alignment", which usually don't have SEQ and QUAL to save disk space. BWA-MEM doesn't output secondary alignment by default. When developing minimap2, I found having secondary alignments are often useful for trouble shooting, so I made them outputted by default. Many tools, such as samtools pileup and depth, ignore secondary alignments.

If more people find this is confusing, I can switch off the feature by default.

What I mean by not breaking duck-typing is that if a user profides his own array class where the result will still be consistent, I dont want to break that. For example, I could imagine you have a class where you overload `__mul__` such that the result goes from `(n, 1)` to `(n,)`. (For example, replace `__mul__` by `np.tensordot`) I don't want to stop users from doing that and make them use only untampered numpy arrays.

But since this project is by now already far more numpy dependent than I originally intended, just looking at `.shape` if it exists and giving a warning it it doesn't match does seem like a good idea.

```python
class ConstrainedNumericalLeastSquares(Minimize):
    def __init__(self, model, *args, **kwargs):
        try:
            super(ConstrainedNumericalLeastSquares, self).__init__(model, *args, **kwargs)
        except TypeError:
            self.constraints = []

    def error_func(self, p, data):
        """
        Least Squares optimization.

        :param p: array of floats for the parameters.
        :param data: data to be provided to ``Variable``'s.
        """
        evaluated_func = self.model(*(list(data) + list(p)))
        chi2 = 0
        for component_name, component_data in evaluated_func._asdict().items():
            ydata = self.dependent_data[component_name]
            sigma_data = self.sigma_data['sigma_{}'.format(component_name)]
            chi2 += np.sum((component_data - ydata)**2/sigma_data**2)
        return chi2

    eval_jacobian = False
```

The change that's causing me problems, and that I didn't describe well earlier, is that previously if a dependency was missing, the error message would tell me which dependency was missing.  Now the error message just tells me which core has a missing dependency.

This means if a core has 10 dependencies I have to go through all 10, one by one to work out which might be causing problems.  Usually it's because I've made a typo in a core name.

I think the message I used to take advantage of was in the SatisfiabilityError message, so when I got a Dependency error I could just scroll up to view that message.  It seems likely that I was depending on SimpleSat error messages that have changed.

I've made an example commit showing a relevant situation:
https://github.com/benreynwar/fusesoc/commit/131e1de29a8e98c798e80d01080ae6e9bc1a7932

Nowhere in the resultant error message is the missing core named.
If people can please double check and make sure I haven't done something horrible like miss a name, that would be great. Then we can cut one more patch release.

My point of view, as a user, may be different from yours, of course :) Ditto re: not being excited about adding more switches, but here goes:

- Most 'normal' users of the Python side of dlib would just do a `pip install` or `conda install` a pre-built tarball, not having to mess with cmake compile flags and the like. This gives them the 'whole thing' if possible so, thus no questions.
- Those who disable things on purpose (e.g. jpeg support) probably do so because they know what they're doing and why they're doing it - in which case it should be no surprise to them that this or that feature ends up being disabled; I would be very surprised if someone purposefully disabled jpeg support and then opened a ticket to complain about, as you've noted above.
- There's parts of dlib (e.g. global optimization) that don't deal with any image related stuff; making libjpeg.so a hard dependency of the Python package is pretty brutal, since now you either have to ensure the boxes you deploy your Python package to have libjpeg installed, or haul it around in every conda environment your package gets installed to, depending on how you're structuring/packaging your Python code. If it wasn't for libjpeg, the only dependency is `numpy` - which makes total sense.
- When building Python package, you could probably wrap module bindings that depend on libjpeg in an ifdef and possibly output a big red cmake warning when building pybind11 bindings (e.g. "face_detection module is disabled due to jpeg_support=0").

In many cases the keyring lib acts as a wrapper around specific keyring implementations - the user could do very little to recover from an error, since the exception would be highly backend-specific.

I think the common usage pattern would be "get this password if you can" and "save this password if you can", but very few users would expect an exception if something strange occurs, and would lead to *lot* of boilerplate code in the caller.

If you are using GZ version 0.4 or later, Guizero widgets have a `tk` attribute which links to the underlying tkinter widget. Since `Box` implements the [tkinter `Frame` widget](http://effbot.org/tkinterbook/frame.htm), you can use [`Frame.configure`](http://effbot.org/tkinterbook/frame.htm#Tkinter.Frame.config-method) to customize it:

```python
import guizero as gz

app = gz.App()
box = gz.Box(app)
box.tk.configure(background="green")

# Any valid color specification can be used here, e.g.
# 'red' or '#000' or '#aabbcc'
```

Otherwise, if you are using an older GZ version, the widgets inherit directly from the underlying tkinter widget:

```
import guizero as gz

app = gz.App()
box = gz.Box(app)
box.configure(background="green")

# Note that I'm leaving out the `tk` here.

You have several ideas packed into one issue, and I actually had to reread several times your post to really understand it all ☺.

## ① Configuration file for "provider repos"

I'm not in favor of configuring remote repositories using extra config files. I chose to setup `git-repo` within the `.gitconfig` to keep things as small and convenient as possible, so I'd rather keep it that way.

It really is a design choice, I want git-repo to be stateless, using the git repository and the remote API as "data store". AFAICT, you can configure the services to have sensible defaults, and do some changes only on what's needed.

Though, nothing would prevent you from doing a little script that would do just that, taking advantage of a feature such as explained below in ②.

## ② Configuration of settings for repositories

There, I do understand your frustration, and there might be a case for extending the API to setup the different remote repositories. Maybe a simple interface that accesses configuration options such as:

```
git hub config list
git hub config get <key>
git hub config set <key> <value>
git hub config <user/repo> list
git hub config <user/repo> get <key>
git hub config <user/repo> set <key> <value>
```

could be a good way to implement a feature to configure the repositories, which would be a single simple interface for all the supported services APIs.

## ③ Add a `git all …` command

I believe there a good idea here, but I'm not sure of which command line API to offer, and how would it be the smartest way to handle that. 

I guess that would be a long term feature that will help unify all the repository services as it's definitely non-trivial to do.

I looked into implementing this a while back but never got around to it and also found it wasn't that straightforward, due to the way the metadata is stored in segments and having to support interleaved data (although it might be best to force loading all data if it is interleaved, as performance would take a big hit in this case). It should definitely be doable though and would be useful.

npTDMS already loads the metadata before loading the actual data so it can allocate a complete array once for a channel and then read directly into that array. For certain TDMS files with many very small segments this turns out to not work that well due to the large amount of memory taken up by the metadata of each segment (eg. see #19), but for most files it seems to work fairly well. If you run tdmsinfo with the `--debug` option it will show the amount of time taken to read metadata and then the time taken to read the actual data. Would probably be useful to show the memory usage of each too.

I think it would make sense to do your escaping on the text node level by default. For that we could simply use `cgi.escape` for all text node types. Doing so by default would be analogous to what most templating engines do.

Btw. `cgi.escape` does exactly what your `escape` function is doing - more info here: https://wiki.python.org/moin/EscapingHtml

I therefore advocate to leave this issue open. The code to do this with json2html is only 3 lines plus some additional tests, so it wouldn't be a big deal to support.

While I'm not the maintainer of json2html, I went and looked up whether list roots are valid JSON according to spec, and indeed they are: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/3833299/can-an-array-be-top-level-json-text . So as long as current test cases still work, I think it's a good idea to support this.

Consistent conventions are important—though never more important than human comprehensibilty.  - different variations of 999 user defined missing values like: (9*)999.(25)(0*)

I don't have any experience with channel objects.  A DateTime object can be created like the other types in `basetypes.py` like this:

    value = DateTime(date=Date(x), time=Time(y))

Where `x` and `y` are appropriate initializer values (in this case 4-tuples). A single `NetworkSecurityPolicy` is an object:

    value = NetworkSecurityPolicy(portId=2, securityLevel='plainTrusted')

An `Array` object can be initialized with a Python array, like this:

    value = ArrayOf(Integer)( [1, 2, 3, 4] )

but after that the array is accessed using BACnet array semantics where the first element of the array (index 0) is the length.  The SequenceOf can also be initialized with a Python array and doesn't have the funky semantics.

You know, on thinking about this further, and investigating a range of possible solutions, I think the bug is in your code rather than in Hypothesis. The problem is that you're not handling the case that sys.path contains unicode, [which is explicitly allowed to do](https://docs.python.org/2/library/sys.html#sys.path). Calling join on a list which contains unicode strings in python 2 will then convert the whole thing to a unicode string, which then breaks on windows because it doesn't want the unicode env variables. This is triggered by Hypothesis, but only because Hypothesis is doing something legit that your code isn't handling.

I'll see what I can do to improve matters, but so far my best solutions only make this "work" as long as your directory names don't actually contain non-ascii codepoints. Either way I think you should fix your code to handle unicode path variables.

It looks like I'm a bit late to the discussion, but I'd like to add a little bit about the recursive implementation vs binding all macaroons with the root.

The idea of binding for a request is to allow the user to take a set of macaroons they've collected, add caveats to the root macaroon that are specific to the request (e.g., limiting it to being valid for a GET http://example.org/route/ in the next 5s from 192.168.1.10) and then binding all macaroons to that route so that none of the macaroons can be used separate from each other.  The root requires others to be valid to verify, and the others are intricately tied to the root.  The same is true even if no additional caveats are added to the root before the request.  The discharge macaroons are not usable with any other root macaroon.  From a security perspective, they cannot fall into the hands of anyone else and be used for anything other than replaying the original request.  And with sufficient caveats, this is much more secure than other schemes.

When you say,

> Binding to the parent, though, prevents end-users from re-using nested discharge macaroons. If macaroon M requires a discharge N which requires a discharge P, then if the user of M requests N from a discharging service, that service can collect P and bind it to N before passing both to the holder of M. This is what I meant by "encapsulation" before.

In this case, the service generating N should not make it rely upon a discharge P without fully trusting the user to hold both N and P.  In a robust implementation, the root key of the third party caveat should be random (I think most libs provide a tool to give a recommended secret---if they don't they should (and I'll add one to libmacaroons if I'm speaking without acting)), and it is securely encrypted using the signature from the caveat immediately preceding the third party caveat.  A poorly implemented discharge service (and I'm betting it's very easy to implement one of these) could be asked to give a discharge N for which the user knows the secret used to construct N.  From there, it can look at all of N's caveats and compute the root for P along the way, stripping the encapsulation.

Of course, this same computation happens during verification too which means that if the user cannot be trusted to hold N, P without some "encapsulation" that protects them (it doesn't), the services trusted by the user where N, P will be used as part of a proof should not be trusted either, for they will be able to undo all the encapsulation.

It is an anti-pattern to have a discharge service generating third-party caveats and providing a discharge macaroon for those caveats, unless the all macaroons are locally constructed.  When this is the case, the macaroons are safe to pass back without any "encapsulation".  The user could just as easy restrict their own context by creating their own proof tree with the same first party caveats---the caveats, after all, are just plain text---and third party secrets that use a different root key to point to copied macaroons.  An invariant of all macaroon verifiers is that adding a caveat will never allow an unauthorized request to become authorized, so there can never be a case where the "original" caveat would succeed but a copy should not.

It's part of this pull request to the Snakemake workflow tool: https://bitbucket.org/snakemake/snakemake/pull-requests/103/static-remote/diff#Lsnakemake/remote/__init__.pyT16

Basically, I needed to conditionally override a few methods of a class in a way that would apply to all subclasses of that class, and the most straightforward way I could think of was to make a proxy that intercepts just those methods and passes through everything else. But Snakemake's internals call `copy.copy` on objects of this class, so my first tests with this solution were throwing ValueError as shown above. Looking at the traceback, it was clear that it was trying to copy the wrapped object's attributes before copying the wrapped object itself, which was causing the exception.

Hi,

I think this indeed falls under the category "intentional", although I'm open to suggestions if there are better ways to deal with this. 

The basic idea behind eigency is to share the underlying representation of a numpy array between Python and C++. This means that somewhere in the process, we need to make explicit which numerical types we are dealing with. In your case, you are passing in an array with float64, and then - from the point of eigency - explicitly asking it to interpret the buffer as a complex128, which will give you the result you describe.

There are various ways around this:

1. You can explicitly specify the type that you expect in the cython definition:
```
 def test_matrix(np.ndarray[np.complex128_t, ndim=2] array):   # instead of just np.ndarray
    return ndarray_copy(_test_matrix(Map[MatrixXcd](array)))
````
Doing so will raise an error if someone passes something that is not of the expected type.

2. Alternatively, you can of course also just do the conversion you describe within cython:
```
def test_matrix(np.ndarray array):
    return ndarray_copy(_test_matrix(Map[MatrixXcd](array.astype(np.complex128))))
```

You could argue that calling Map[MatrixXcd] on an array of type float64 should be illegal. As far as I can see, that would however require us to implement template specializations for every type of Map object, where it for each type is hardcoded which python dtypes are allowed. This is in principle doable, but it would mostly be useful for providing an error, but this is already supported by option 1 above. Of course, the template specializations could in principle do the appropriate conversion to a different type automatically - but that would have surprising effects for people who expect that the memory storage is shared between Python and C++ (which it would not be if the storage type changes behind the scenes). 
Long story short: In Eigency we leave it up to the designer of the interface to specify how specific the interface should be. You can should yourself in the foot this way - but c++ programmers are used to that :)

@john-kurkowski I'm confused by this.

Using one of @evanv 's helpful examples, of a case where doing two `.strip()` calls works: `' ads.adiquity.com . '` Not sure this is a use-case we should support. As you know I'm sure ...

> It's a little-known fact, but fully-qualified (unambiguous) DNS domain names have a dot at the end. 

(Pulling a source out of a hat... http://www.dns-sd.org/trailingdotsindomainnames.html)

We remove the trailing dot simply because it is not useful (makes it annoying when you call `my_extract_result.split('.')` ) ... and in many contexts it doesn't communicate special information. It's implicit. So we strip the trailing dot: `.rstrip('.')`

I think there's no need for us to remove a space between the top-level domain (aka public suffix) and the trailing dot. It should just never be given as an input.

I think 

```
            .rstrip(".") \
            .strip()
```

is the best way.

Calling `.strip()` in the middle just seems excessive. Sorry to drag this on!

I encountered this issue and it turned out to be a configuration issue the led the wrong driver to be used with `pyodbc.connect` when using a DSN.

Upon investigation, I found `SQLSRV32.DLL` was always incorrectly bring used as the driver, despite my configuration specifying the ODBC driver. I hand-patched in my own connection string in the `get_new_connection` method and the error went away and the driver was the appropriate `MSODBCSQL` dll.

As it turns out, when you specify the dsn option, the 'driver' option from the configuration is ignored entirely. For some unknown reason, the driver specified in my DSN file was not being used. The ultimate solution appears to be to simply not use a DSN connection and provide all the options for the connection manually via the 'extra_params' option.

Although theory I agree that__getattr__ should behave as __getitem__, in practice they did not. This is because in 
    row.xxx
    row['yyy']
xxx is constraint to be alphanumeric and therefore has to be a field (and we can optimize for this) or a table (in result of joins) while yyy can be an expression (from _extra) or a 'table.field'. I do not object to having a single underlying implementation unless we make it slower by ignoring the fact that they they serve different purposes. Has anybody test the speed of the code after the change? Some time ago we spent huge amount of time refactoring this code since it is a web2py bottle neck.

1. `stdint.h` is a C99 standard head file, but VS2008 has poor support for C99, and `stdint.h` is missing in VS2008 by default.
2. `stdint.h` is included in `zmq.h`, which is included by  `buildutils/vers.c`, so compiling `buildutils/vers.c' with msvc complaining `Can not find file stdint.h`.
3. To resolve this issue, a customized `stdint.h` was added in commit https://github.com/zeromq/pyzmq/commit/bdccfb646ba739da573781007ce86490512917e5, which include `stdint.hpp` from bundled zeromq.
4. However, if we build pyzmq in non-bundle mode, no zeromq source code will be downloaded, thus `#include ../../bundled/zeromq/src/stdint.hpp` will fail.So we should not try to provide our customized `stdint.h` in such case.

Hi @mjmare,

Short story:
If you cannot control when the server and client starts in your environment, then I would recommend you to add the following code into your client startup:

```
def wait_for_server(host, port, timeout=10):
    port = port or 7474
    running = False
    t = 0
    while not running and t < timeout:
        try:
            s = create_simple_connection((host, port)) # or try to create a driver
        except IOError:
            sleep(1)
            t += 1
        else:
            s.close()
            running = True

    if not running:
        raise RuntimeError("Server %s:%d did not become available in %d seconds" % (host, port, timeout))
```

Long story:

Before the server starts, the port is not open for connecting. The driver's attempt to create connection will be immediately rejected. In this case, a connection dose not even get started. The `connection_timeout` will only be honored if your connection is connected but not yet fully finished to create (due to some high load in your server). For more details explanation, you probably need to read more about TCP and OS about how they handle sockets.

I verified that the config parameter `connection_timeout` is passed down correctly in the code. There is some checks for certain config parameters such as encryption config. But it only happens near the place where they are used in the code. We will take your suggestion to valid them as earlier as possible in future driver versions.

Thanks again.

basically I have something like this:
```python
def relay_wrapper(func: asyncio.coroutine, num_parallel: int):
    """
    Will wrap `func` and run `num_parallel` in parallel, returning result from first
    that completes, cancelling others.

    :param func: async method to call
    :param num_parallel: number of calls to run in parallel
    :return:
    """
    async def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        done, pending = await asyncio.wait([func(*args, **kwargs) for _ in range(num_parallel)], return_when=asyncio.FIRST_COMPLETED)
        for task in pending:
            await cancel_if_running(task)

        # find first non-exception result
        for task in done:
            if not task.exception():
                return task.result()

        # otherwise return first result
        return done.pop().result()

    return wrapper
```

and use it like this, suppose `get_object_` is equivalent to botocore's s3 client's `get_object`

self.get_object = relay_wrapper(self.get_object, num_parallel_gets)

I think I'm onto something, I stopped with pdb to watch at the type of `proc.stderr`, and found out that `proc.stderr.mode is 'rb'`, so it's binary buffering, and it's unlikely to have universal line ending enabled.

Then I went on to read [subprocess documentation](https://docs.python.org/3.5/library/subprocess.html#subprocess.Popen.communicate) where it says:

> Popen.stderr
>
>    If the stderr argument was PIPE, this attribute is a readable stream object as returned by open(). Reading from the stream provides error output from the child process. **If the universal_newlines argument was True, the stream is a text stream, otherwise it is a byte stream.** If the stderr argument was not PIPE, this attribute is None.

so I guess it's worth trying with `Popen(..., universal_newlines=True)`!

I mentioned this somewhere else, but can’t find my own comment at the moment, so I’m going to reiterate it briefly.

setup.py is not actually the only way to install packages (as you might already be aware, given the mention of pyproject.toml), just the default way. PEP 517 defines an interface for build systems to work with pip, and you can use build system declarations to replace setuptools (which uses setup.py) with another tool to specify how your package should be installed. Flit is currently the most prominent alternative.

“How can I install dependencies in Pipfile.lock from setup.py” is, therefore, a misled question. You can’t, and  the real question should be “how can I declare my package to use Pipfile.lock as my dependency specification” instead. The answer is simple—you need an alternative build system that works that way, and declare you package to use it in pyproject.toml.

So how can you that build system I mentioned? You can’t, because it does not exist. See, Pipfile and Pipenv are not tightly-coupled; the former is a specification, and you can freely build libraries to interface with it. Pipenv, however, is only a tool that uses the specification, to handle application dependencies. It does not handle packaging Python libraries. And no-one has built a tool to do what you want—yet, until you build it. PEP 517 isn’t very difficult to implement, and Flit already figured out most of the things, so you can just ~copy-paste~ take inspiration from it.

Or, to put it short: Pipenv doesn’t do what you want, and likely won’t ever. Do it yourself.

@FRidh - I think there are two different ways of seeing setuptools etc.  One is the perspective of the system administrator, who has full control of all Python package deployments.  From that point of view, it makes sense to think about trying to specify setuptools versions, avoid upgrades when possible and so on. From the sysadmin perspective, it isn't a very great concern that a release causes widespread breakage, because the sysadmin can diagnose and fix it quickly enough - it's just a couple of hours of their working day every now and then.  However, I'm thinking of the second perspective, which is that of the default or ordinary user .  For that user, I'd like to do everything I can to make sure that they just never get a weird crash from using standard tools like setuptools.  In my experience (as someone who teaches students and new programmers) - that can cause a lot of confusion, and tends to repel people from the community ("it's too fragile"). If we can do some low-maintenance continuous integration to make a crash less likely, I think that's a very good investment.

@pganssle there is one problem which can't be solved at post-processing: handling of relative dates like '2 hours ago'. On forums it is common to have relative and absolute dates mixed - older dates are absolute, recent posts/comments have relative dates. Because dateparser returns absolute dates, there must be a way to tell dateparser "this website uses this timezone" in order to parse such relative dates correctly. If you parse these dates as relative to server time or to UTC then you need to convert absolute dates to server time or UTC (otherwise returned dates will be incompatible) - and you need server timezone information for that. 

I agree that all other timezone processing is less critical because it can be handled by an end user as a post-processing step.

> The more we simplify the commit message, merging process and engineering bureaucracy, the more contributions will emerge

The goal behind our QA practices is not the red tape; it is to make the final product more robust.

> Therefore, Co-authored-by is a valid signature that we should keep (as optional, of course), but I feel that you are agreeing with me on this one.

Yes, we fully agree on this one; we probably disagree about the usefulness of (optional) Tested-by/Reviewed-by signatures. 

My point about `git blame` was the following.  I presume that people don't really use `git blame` in its "raw" state, but rather through their code editors.  (If not, then they should :smile:)  Here, one has richer "blame" functionality at one's fingertips.  E.g. I find it useful that I can do `C-x v g l RET` and see almost everything about the given line of code: not only when it went to the codebase and who authored it (the usual "blame"), but also who tested-it and who reviewed-it (thanks to QA signatures), and what was the announced user-level purpose (thanks to #2856 labels).  This is one of the advantages of having rich commit log messages, as opposed to storing this information elsewhere.  People have all the information at their fingertips in their code editors, as opposed to having to look things up through independent news file or in an external issue tracker.  (The cons being that the git commit message is "set in stone" after merge, but we discussed these pros/cons in #2856 already.)

`EvalFactor.code` looks used to me?

Forward compatibility: It'd be good if trying to load a new pickle in an old version at least gave an error? But we can always deal with that later at the time we decide to change something in a breaking fashion. (I guess if one wants to get really fancy one could put a version number field in every pickle so that old versions will not just error out, but also be able to display a comprehensible error message - "This version of patsy is too old..." -- but this is definitely an extra credit kind of thing.)

The testing strategy also looks good to me. I guess the one subtlety will be that if we add new stuff in a future release then we'll need to have some way to add new test objects that are only present in newer pickle dumps, while still keeping the old pickle dumps.

One possible approach: represent the test objects as little bits of constructor code -- `"EvalFactor(...)"`, etc. -- and then have the version snapshot script dump both the pickles themselves and also the code used to create the objects that they should be compared to. (And then there can also be a test that loops over the same list for round-trips: `for code in TEST_OBJECT_CODES: obj = eval(code); assert pickle.loads(pickle.dumps(obj)) == obj`)

> less management

I think the only new management we have to do is calling stop_standing_subprocess() right?

> less complexity

Actually if we have codepaths for both, there's more complexity because we have to implement and test both ways of starting snippets.

Also (most importantly for me) the complexity will be exposed to the user, because now they'll have to know to load their snippet differently than before if/when it gets UIAutomator calls.

And there's no good way for us to produce a good error to help them here since the symptom will be that UIAutomator.getDevice() will just return `null` on the Java side. That's not something we can catch/wrap.

> less code, less work

More code and work to support both, see above

> Why can't you name the Patroni slots in an idiosyncratic way so that we know which ones they are?

1. It would be not backward compatible.
2. Anybody can spoil it.


> Postgres doesn't require any particular naming for slots, so we could use any name we want.

Well, first of all, slot name can't be longer than 63 characters. Also it may only contain lower case letters, numbers, and the underscore character: https://github.com/postgres/postgres/blob/master/src/backend/replication/slot.c#L164-L210

That's why we have a function: [slot_name_from_member_name](https://github.com/zalando/patroni/blob/master/patroni/postgresql.py#L53-L66)

You have to configure the username and the password for pg_rewind first (i.e. https://github.com/zalando/patroni/blob/master/postgres0.yml). Create a pg_rewind section in the patroni configuration file and specify there the superuser username and the password, i.e.
pg_rewind:
    username: postgres
    password: pwforpostgres

I assume you have set either the wal_log_hints or enabled the checksum, because this is a prerequisite to running pg_rewind.

I don't know if you'd want to use qinspect in tests, I believe Django's `TransactionTestCase.assertNumQueries()` may be a better tool (you can assert that exactly some specified number of queries were executed while executing  a block of code).

That said, since qinspect can add headers carrying the sql execution data to the repsonse, you could examine/assert on those headers. It won't be very elegant, but it should work. If there's a good use case not covered by the `assertNumQueries` that qinspect can fill, I'm open to adding functionality to it to make it easier for use in tests.

# Manticore Initial State Customization Proposal v2

## initial state registration API

First argument to `Manticore` is either:
- `str`:  path to binary (convenience/legacy interface)
- `State: initial State object of analysis. this is the initial state registration API. API users would not really be using this, since the `State` is too low level of an object for them to be manually constructing and dealing with. They will primarily be using the platform classmethods

## manticore initialization API changes

To support initializing Manticore for different platforms cleanly, classmethods corresponding to platforms are proposed.

```
m = Manticore.evm()
m = Manticore.linux('/binary', argv=[], envp=[])
m = Manticore.weird_embedded('firmware.blob', hw_config=dict(dict with some configs))
```

For API compatibility, and as a convenience interface for Linux (which is expected to be a primary use case), we will maintain the conventional Manticore constructor

```
m = Manticore('/binary', argv)
```

## initial state customization

A hook will be provided for after Manticore's internal state init, but before analysis has begun (before instructions are executed). In this hook, platform and CPU apis can be called as needed in order to perform custom state initialization

```
@m.init
def inithook(state):
  platform = state.platform
  cpu = state.cpu
```

## API for dynamically enqueueing arbitrary states

We will export `m.enqueue(state)` as an undocumented api to dynamically enqueue states. Users should typically not need to do this, as they should use the classmethods, however it is required for evm changes we wish to introduce. this api is relevant to this rfc as it is relevant to initializing manticore with States.

This way `Row.commit()` does have to worry about sending up to 3 API requests to the RPC methods `MutateRow` (no filter), `CheckAndMutateRow` (filter) and `ReadModifyWriteRow` (append).

`Client.load_table_from_storage` takes *one or more* source URIs (that is what `*soure_uris` means in python). E.g.:

```python
job = client.load_table_from_storage(
    'load-job-123', my_table_object,
    'gs://my-bucket-name/table_one',
    'gs://my-bucket-name/table_two')
```

Any annotation request contains an instance of `google.cloud.vision_v1.types.Image`, which can have either its `source` property set (containing the URL of the image, e.g. in GCS) or its `content` property (containing the actual bytes of the image).  So, for instance:

```python
from google.cloud import vision
from google.cloud.vision import enums
from google.cloud.vision import types

features = [
    types.Feature(type=enums.Feature.Type.TEXT_DETECTION),
    types.Feature(type=enums.Feature.Type.FACE_DETECTION),
]

requests = []
for filename in ['foo.png', 'bar.jpg', 'baz.gif']:
    with open(filename, 'rb') as image_file:
        image = types.Image(
            contents = image_file.read())
    request = types.AnnotateImageRequest(
        image=image, features=features)
    requests.append(request)

response = client.batch_annotate_images(requests)

for annotation_response in response.responses:
   do_something_with(annotation_response)
```

The behavior I've implemented is creating a new line, leaving the original line (the line on which the user Ctrl-C'd) as is. The way I've implemented it is to:

- The user hits Ctrl-C while on line 1.
- Save the command in line 1.
- Tell the command line to execute an empty command (does nothing).
- Sets the string of line 1 to the original value

It doesn't do any worker-killing, it simply moves the user to the next line.

@tarsisazevedo what I had in mind (and I think we can get a more general solution) was something that the person writing the wrapper could be in control. So the magic would happen in the `TapiocaAdapter`. Doing this on the `Tapioca` class will make us tied to a single way of handling errors that might not be the way many APIs do it.

`process_response` is the place to handle errors: https://github.com/vintasoftware/tapioca-wrapper/blob/master/tapioca/adapters.py#L54
It's also the place where we raise exceptions. What I had in mind was that it was possible to pass the specific error message there. Your approach of looking for `error`in the response may be the default implementation (and should happen after we get the request data https://github.com/vintasoftware/tapioca-wrapper/blob/master/tapioca/adapters.py#L58), but clients will be able to overwrite it and look for the error somewhere else in the response.

> whitespace, funny characters and capital letters don't add any gain, but only hassles.

Whitespace and funny characters: no. Whitespace and funny characters aren't
valid identifier characters in mainstream programming languages (Python,
Javascript, Go, etc) and thus I agree and see little reason to allow them in
WAMP URIs.

But capital letters? Not only are capital letters valid identifier characters in
every single WAMP implementation language enumerated here:

  * http://wamp-proto.org/implementations/

But hell: in Erlang, identifiers *have* to start with a capital letter.

  http://erlang.org/doc/reference_manual/expressions.html#id80984

Thus, support for capital letters engenders an immediate, tangible gain: WAMP
URIs, e.g. exported RPC functions, no longer have to needlessly and confusingly
mangle camelCase identifiers and function names.

That's exactly the problem I ran headlong into. I have to confusingly register
this function

```python
async def constructClientPayloadOnConnect(self, conn):
   pass
```

under a different, snake_case name.

```python
@register('construct_client_payload_on_connect')
async def constructClientPayloadOnConnect(self, conn):
   pass
```

I had one function name. Now I have two. Because of this restriction, the number
of function identifiers every developers has to remember and reason about
doubles. And I'm clearly not the only one to be bitten by this:

  https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/autobahnws/mkjF21Fb8ow

So there's an immediately gain for the allowance of capital letters: camelCase
identifiers and functions aren't mangled.

WAMP should encourage consistent code. Unfortunately this restriction does the
opposite.

Let's fix it!

I have a vague memory of seeing things like this before (mind you this was 4+ years ago now (!?)) where if you use a too big mask and have dense particles you get a bias towards the center due to the edges of the CoM mask picking up weight from the neighboring particles.  Because they are farther out they have greater effect per count on the CoM and if you have lots of lines of particles you will get this bias as the wings will a) dominate and b) balance each other.

Also check that you are not finding false particles in the empty spaces.  If you get a small local maximum in a region that is basically constant, the CoM will be centered on the pixel with the local max.

OK. I narrowed it down. It's caused by `mkdir_p_sudo_safe`. I forgot to mention, that I have users for various tasks, into which I login from main user with
```
sudo -u marek -i
```
This opens new login shell under the specified user. I've seen many people use this practice.

I don't really understand what business does Anaconda have with sudo but I suggest to remove this behavior as it breaks legitimate user setups. Anaconda probably expects that sudo is only used to become root, which is far from truth.

```
    if isdir(path): 
        return 
    base_dir = dirname(path) 
    if not isdir(base_dir): 
        mkdir_p_sudo_safe(base_dir) 
    log.trace('making directory %s', path) 
    mkdir(path) 
    if not on_win and os.environ.get('SUDO_UID') is not None: 
        uid = int(os.environ['SUDO_UID']) 
        gid = int(os.environ.get('SUDO_GID', -1)) 
        log.trace("chowning %s:%s %s", uid, gid, path) 
        os.chown(path, uid, gid) 
```

Obviously, the user to which I login via sudo doesn't have permissions to `chown()` files under previous account credentials.

Hgnc (genenames.org) have a single curated transcript which is the de facto standard transcript for setting up single gene tests, with the refSeq mildly preferred over ensembl - and a REST to retrieve them (http://www.genenames.org/help/rest-web-service-help). They don't make explicit claims about disease causation - this is about standardized nomenclature.
Still, for the genes I checked, they did get the preferred, relevant transcript.  I'd say this makes an acceptable fallback, but perhaps "HGNC transcript" is a better name for it than canonical.

Ensembl have a well defined "canonical transcript" (http://www.ensembl.org/Help/Glossary?id=346) themselves. Often, but not always what we want.

Background: VCF 4.2 and earlier files were ASCII only, however VCF 4.3 allows UTF-8 encoded VCF files with non-ASCII characters allowed in INFO and FORMAT value fields.  Certain characters must be percent encoded, including percent signs, colons and semi-colons.  (e.g., the HGVS strings in your file are also going to cause errors due to the colons.)

To address this question:

> So my question is: Is this expected? Eg "--older-than" does not work with "--all-indices" and I'd have to use regex to delete all indices older than 30 days for example.

The answer is still with [the `--timestring` flag documentation](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/timestring.html#_flags_19):

```
curator delete indices --timestring '%Y.%m.%d'
2015-06-19 09:54:53,728 INFO      Job starting: delete indices
2015-06-19 09:54:53,740 INFO      Pruning Kibana-related indices to prevent accidental deletion.
You are using --timestring without --older-than or --newer-than.
This could result in actions being performed on all indices matching %Y.%m.%d
Press CTRL-C to exit Curator before the timer expires:
10 ^C
Aborted!
```

In your above example, using the `--timestring` flag triggered the countdown, even if it erroneously was going to select all indices.  Regexes are implied.  Using `--timestring %Y.%m.%d` will match _all_ indices that match that time pattern, which in my case would be all `logstash-YYYY.MM.dd` and `.marvel-YYYY.MM.dd` indices.

If so, it should be possible for the CLI to transform the object by selecting only the fields listed in the `--select` option (aren't Python objects treatable as maps, similar to JS objects?), or are there nasty edge cases?

I'm no longer consuming Batch CLI output, so this isn't an issue for me any longer, but I still think the behaviour is surprising - but if other people aren't having problems with it, then I'm okay if you want to close this off.

This is so long ago, but basing this from memory and not code. I used the `next_marker` to traverse and find a list of folders.

For files smaller than 64MB, the service would calculate the overall content MD5. Unless there is a compelling argument for having this feature, the overall content MD5 calculation will not be implemented in the SDK, as it has significant performance impact, and the user could easily calculate the MD5 on their own.

Here is some sample code kindly provided by @ajith-k:

```python
from hashlib import md5
from base64 import b64encode
from azure.storage.blob import (
       BlockBlobService,
       ContentSettings
)
 
def getmd5(fname):
    hash_md5 = md5()
    with open(fname, "rb") as f:
        for chunk in iter(lambda: f.read(4096), b""):
            hash_md5.update(chunk)
    return b64encode(hash_md5.digest()).decode('utf-8')
 
block_blob_service = BlockBlobService(account_name='<redacted>', account_key='<redacted>')
block_blob_service.create_blob_from_path(
    'container_name',
    'file_name',
    'file_path',
    content_settings=ContentSettings(content_md5=getmd5('file_path')))
```

Some diagnoses:

1. the `+` was introduced by using the wrong escape function (`tornado.escape.url_escape`, which is for URL query parameters by default, not url components)
2. Putting a name in quotes means it shouldn't be escaped, but it also has to be Latin-1 if you do that.
3. [RFC 6266](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6266) explains how to specify filename encodings properly (url escapes were the right thing to do, we just did them wrong)

#2767 should implement filename encoding correctly. I've tested it on Safari, Chrome, and Firefox.

Great discussion, and I appreciate all the examples.

> I'm puzzled as to why gaining the ability to expressly unset a field would be so terrible.

I don't think it's a bad idea. It would solve the problem with partial models. We would see fewer surprises. It would be a net plus to the library, and everyone would be happier.

My argument is that it *only* solves the problem with partial models, and there *might* be other ways to address it.

> generic Python objects don't auto-initialize their attributes either. And neither do they equate None and "not set":

I think the main purpose of introducing a concept of Schema into a language like Python is to have something closer to classes/structs in a traditional static language, with predefined and rigid structures and fixed "slots" for attributes. In those languages - and in other frameworks with schema like Django - you don't distinguish between `None`/`NULL` with unset slot. So the behavior of Schematics is not inherently broken. What's broken is the behavior of default value (that's for another discussion), and that we (ab)use Schematics model in supporting the concept of partial.

If we draw an analogy from between this and relational databases, which also have the concept of schema (and default and required-ness, etc), one could bend it so he can insert a partial row. It will work, but at a cost.

If we stop thinking about partial model for a minute, does the concept of unsetting a value have other values? On the other hand, is there value in making Schematics behave more like other systems with the concept of schemas (and default and required-ness), particularly from a learner perspective?

Comparing with what the JS SDK does,

- the JS SDK only generates and uploads 5 one-time keys at a time (https://github.com/matrix-org/matrix-js-sdk/blob/master/src/crypto/index.js#L284..L288) because generating the OTKs can take a long time.  It should be faster in the Python SDK because he olm library is running native code, but it might be worth doing some timings to see if it should also limit the number of keys that it generates at a time
- the JS SDK doesn't update the one-time-keys until after it has caught up on toDevice messages (https://github.com/matrix-org/matrix-js-sdk/blob/master/src/crypto/index.js#L943..L946 and https://github.com/matrix-org/matrix-js-sdk/pull/376) to avoid deleting one-time keys that are about to be used. It doesn't look like you're handling toDevice messages yet (I guess that comes in a later PR), so this may or may not be a concern
- it looks like it tries to upload as many one-time keys as possible, which I guess is the same as setting the keys_threshold parameter to 1, so maybe that should be the default value at https://github.com/matrix-org/matrix-python-sdk/pull/237/files#diff-b0fcf5d6f2d0d6eda3db1946fac9eb4aR42

However, I also think that, at least, the `collect-metrics` hook is actually more like actions in that they express specific intent as to what code path should be invoked and that it should return a result.  That said, I think we're still missing the feature I would consider necessary to do this correctly (stable handlers), so I'm ok with moving forward with this and refactoring as needed in the future.  This really should only affect one (albeit base) layer and should be easy enough to make backwards compatible when / if we do refactor it.

@victorhaggqvist, there is currently no way to do exactly what you want. The client needs root for two things:

1. To access the default directories.
2. To bind to port 80/443.

You can fix 1 on the command line. `--config-dir`, `--work-dir`, and `--logs-dir`, can all be changed to directories that the current user can access to mitigate that problem. The certificates are stored in directories under `config-dir`.

You can fix 2 by using the `webroot` or `manual` plugins (see [this](https://letsencrypt.readthedocs.org/en/latest/using.html#plugins)).

To get the cert in your current directory, there is one thing you can do but it requires some extra work. You can pass the client a CSR with `--csr` that will be used to generate your certificate (see `letsencrypt --help certonly` for more info). If you do this and don't specify any other paths than the ones mentioned above, the resulting certificate will be saved in your current directory by default.

I'm sorry there's no way to do exactly what you're looking for, but I hope these suggestions help.

I thought about it again and I can think of a way forward.

Another reason why I was reluctant to implement the feature was that I wanted it to be configurable (... so that persons like myself can choose not to use it :-)) but did not want to have a magic system like metakernel has (see discussion at #72).  However, I just realized that you could instead configure this via environment variables: the user would have to do something like `setenv('MK_INLINE_PLOT', '<some-string>')` (`MK_` = MATLAB Kernel -- I should rename `CONNECT_MATLAB` to `MK_CONNECT` as well) to configure it from within the notebook, or could set it globally in their `.bash_profile` (or equivalent).

From the point of view of the implementation, you'd have to patch [`do_execute`](https://github.com/anntzer/matlab_kernel/blob/master/matlab_kernel/_kernel.py#L119) as follows:
- Fetch the value of the environment variable relatively early (via `self._call("getenv", "MK_INLINE_PLOT")`, and, if set, parse it.
- If it is set, set the relevant options, as in https://github.com/Calysto/matlab_kernel/blob/master/matlab_kernel/kernel.py#L190
- If it is set, run the relevant blob after execution finished, as in https://github.com/Calysto/matlab_kernel/blob/master/matlab_kernel/kernel.py#L75 (you'd have to inline the definition of `self.Display`, which comes from `metakernel`).

What's a good format for `MK_INLINE_PLOT`?  If non-empty, it has to contain the figure size, figure resolution and figure format, so it may as well be a json string (that gets merged with some reasonable defaults), e.g. `'{"size": [8, 6], "resolution": 150, "format": "png"}'`.

Now you just get to implement it :-)

> If I'm understanding correctly, neither class nor instance methods imply any sort of state.

They don't _imply_ state, but they carry the possibility.  Fundamentally I don't trust future-us to not be dumb.  Functional-style code makes that somewhat easier (for me at least).

> These classes in particular should be considered immutable (though I'm not sure the easiest way to actually enforce this),

I've been working on a _very_ similar problem recently (for `relativedelta`-like objects); let's discuss in a separate thread.  The short answer is "do `__init__` in cython, and pray you never have to worry about pickling".

> I tend to avoid staticmethod because it's more or less pointless.

Yah, they behave like module-level functions but are in the class namespace and can be overriden at the instance/class level.  It's mostly a organizational/encapsulation/style thing to loudly declare "these functions go together".

> In this case, all the class methods actually use the fact that they are class methods, since they are retrieving class attributes.

In e.g. `_parse_tzstr`, `cls._TIME_SEP` is used.  In the function-based approach I advocate, that would be passed as an argument (probably a kwarg with a default).

> Class methods get access to class attributes and AFAIK there's no real overhead to including them

I think that's accurate in python-land, not so sure about cython-land.  Let's not worry about this micro-optimization just yet.

> since we could privately inherit it to create isoparser variants.

For methods that are likely to be overridden I'm totally on board.

One thing to note, I would be *somewhat* cautious about using `rrule` with non-monotonic time zones like this. It's *mostly* fine, but the problem is that `rrule` will not try to resolve imaginary or ambiguous times, so for all ambiguous times you'll always get the DST side (no way to specify which side it lands on), and `rrule` will erroneously generate imaginary times, e.g.:

```python
from datetime import datetime
from dateutil import tz, rrule

dtstart = datetime(2017, 3, 12, 1, 30, tzinfo=tz.gettz('America/New_York'))

rr = rrule.rrule(dtstart=dtstart,
                 freq=rrule.HOURLY,
                 count=3)

for dt in rr:
    print('%s: %s' % (str(dt), tz.datetime_exists(dt)))
# 2017-03-12 01:30:00-05:00: True
# 2017-03-12 02:30:00-04:00: False
# 2017-03-12 03:30:00-04:00: True
```

I think that strictly speaking the RFC says that all imaginary datetime should be simply *skipped*, so this is a bug in `dateutil`'s `rrule` implementation and that bug may be fixed in the future (possibly soon).

If you want finer control over how these edge cases are handled, it's better to generate naive datetimes and then attach a time zone to them as desired.

> It's not really fair to call 2.7 10+ years old, or if one does, to use that as a criticism.

I don't mean it as a criticism, I just mean that people using Python 2 probably are more concerned with stability than with getting the latest features. `dateutil`'s core feature set is quite stable, just like Python 2's. When we drop support, it doesn't mean that users from Python 2 can't use `dateutil` anymore, it just means they can only use the perfectly good `3.x` branch, or they have to write their own backports of new features.

> At the very least, it would be considerate to document the hard technical reasons why py2 support would overburden a project to the point of dropping it before PSF support ends.

Honestly, the "hard technical reasons" aren't that important. At some point Python 2 support *will* end. I think many of the people participating in the [Python 3 statement](https://python3statement.org/) are looking to make that transition *easier* by acting as canaries in the coal mine. For example, IPython's early dropping of Python 2.x support in the 6.x branch has already shown a lot of weakpoints in systems that don't support the `Requires-Python` metadata in a relatively harmless way, ahead of the onslaught of packages dropping Python 2 support. When `matplotlib` drops support for 2.x in their 3.0 release this summer, I think we'll see a lot more of these bugs cropping up as well. I think it's important to give the ecosystem time to cope with this and develop idioms around the transition, rather than everyone coordinating on January 1, 2020 as the "we can drop Python 2" day.

I noticed that in the case of missing timestamps, the most current one will be returned:

> Return a compact ISO8601 timestamp (UTC timezone) indicating when the file was last modified
> 
> Note: if the layer does not contain a timestamp, and the mode is 'r+', a new timestamp will be set and returned.
> Otherwise, the current time in UTC will be returned.

I propose to modify this to instead return `19700101T000000Z` (in other words: [Unix Time](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unix_time) if timestamps are missing and the file is opened in read-only mode. Two reasons for this:

- Due to how `last_modified` is self-initialising, the only context in which the timestamp will remain missing, is when the loom file is consistently opened in read-only mode. This implies that the content of the file will stay unmodified, removing the need to update the cache.
- Returning the most recent UTC does not allow us distinguish missing timestamps from very recent ones (calling timestamp again would return a later timestamp, after all). By comparison, Unix Time is a clear marker for missing time data, allowing us to take special actions if desired (we can always decide to fall back to a current time stamp if needed).

Most likely the only way to implement this would be to instantiate the parent serializer and serialize the given model. Then ready the relation from serializer.data.

instance = self.get_object()

serializer.data['name-of-relation']

Basic idea is that when you start dragging the slider or changing it in any way you call `stop()`. As the position changes you call `set(position)` updating the UI via the timer. Then when you are done sliding things around you trigger a seek against mopidy but leave to timer stopped. Then when you get the `position-changed` event you call `timer.set(position).start()`

This is at least how I've gone about in an other project which uses the media-progress-timer. The possible shortfall in this is that you don't get the playing state and the position in the same event, which we need to get fixed in mopidy. But for now you could probably work around that.

> why not simply point to a cloud-based storage location, such as the S3 bucket in your list

Well, firstly because we (@ebenolson, actually) pay for S3 and secondly because I'd prefer to include the official and probably most permanent location. Not sure if the costs for S3 would noticeable, but people use the example code as a basis for other experiments and so the link could spread quite a bit.

It's the first time I've seen LeCun's website being down, by the way -- from my (limited) experience, it's highly available. That's why I propose to just leave it the way it is for now.

As a compromise, we could just list the alternative download locations in a comment so they're easy to swap in (this wouldn't help with continuous integration, but at least make it easier for end users).

> I don't have time now to work on a new ordereddict. Do one of you can search on the web if a good implementation already exist?

The most sane way would be to update [Theano's copy](https://github.com/Theano/Theano/blob/master/theano/compat/python2x.py) of the MIT-licensed [ordereddict backport on PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/ordereddict) to use `weakref.proxy` for the backward links, as in Python 3.4, and possibly contribute the change back to PyPI (I've already asked its maintainer @sah if he's interested, but haven't heard back yet).
The backport is almost the same as the code in Python 2.7 (which isn't that surprising as they're both by Raymond Hettinger: http://bugs.python.org/issue5397). I'm not sure if we'd run into license issues by just porting the `weakref.proxy` idea from the Python 3.4 codebase to the backport? There's hardly any design choices anyway... if we want to use weak references, then `weakref.proxy` is the easiest way, and we need to turn the linked-list nodes from `list` instances into objects because `weakref` does not support `list`. That's exactly what has happened in Python 3.4.

I agree this one isn't ideal, but I'm not confident my other idea for an API is more ideal, yet.

First, `family` exists to make the URL more useful for `socket` interoperation. Several `socket` APIs take a `family` argument, and this exactly maps to that. Furthermore, if `family` is `socket.AF_INET6`, then `to_text` will wrap the hostname in square brackets, per the standard. But square brackets are a no-go for socket APIs. 

In short, with the (`host`, `port`, `family`) split arrangement, `URL` is out-of-the-box usable with `socket`.

---

As for alternatives, one option I considered before hyperlink and even now, is to allow square brackets in the value for `host`, and use those as the IPv6 flag. There are a couple downsides to this:

* Users have to manually wrap/unwrap square brackets (or use a separate/new API)
* Doesn't give any indication as to whether the address parsed as IPv4

But at least it would be less likely to have family and host get out of sync. Right now,  to .replace()` a URL from an IPv4 to an IPv6 host will also require passing `family=socket.AF_INET6` (because of the colons). As I type this, I think that may be why I made `parse_host` part of the public API (#25). I think I preferred that slightly to manual IPv6 guessing and square bracket wrapping.

What do you think?

There is not much we can do about this since this is how python's module resolution works. Graphene is based off [graphql-core](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core) which is a port of the reference graphql server implementation [graphql-js](https://github.com/graphql/graphql-js). `graphql-core` installs itself as `graphql` so if you name a file `graphql.py` in your project python will pick that up first and any further imports to `graphql` will reference your local file and not the library `graphql-core`.

To avoid this issue I can only suggest that you name your graphql implementation as something other than the literal name "graphql" (or "graphene" for that matter).

Just to be clear, the problem is that the conan_package_tools API is not stable, so it breaks the user side scripts when it is updated, like in this case.

I.e., at .travis/install.sh it executes "pip install conan_package_tools".

If conan_package_tools doesn't have a stable or semantic version stable API, then it should be locked down to a specific version, and allowing it to be updated to a potentially incompatible version, by design.

Of course this is user managed code, but your .travis/install.sh example is the gold standard, and it will be largely used like this, and every time you break API, people will have their builds failing and come here to report that as a bug.

So I suggest:

- Lock down to a specific version
- Print a message warning if version is not the latest, telling the developer that he needs to consider upgrading manually.

Here's how I've been using live integration tests with the other ESPs:

* Most ESPs *don't* offer a sandbox. So the tests run truly live. The recipients are always \@example.com (or SendGrid's special "sink domain" for their tests) to avoid test email arriving in unexpected places.
* I maintain an Anymail test account and validated sender domain for each ESP (that doesn't make this cost-prohibitive). The API keys for those accounts are available in the Travis environment on the master branch. (Travis security blocks those environment vars in most pull requests, so the live tests won't run from your PR until I merge it. But you can set the vars in your environment to your own account for local testing.)
* Most ESPs *don't* return any useful send status from their send APIs. (Email is inherently async, and you have to implement webhooks if you want to know actual send status.) So the live integration tests are mainly making sure the ESP API hasn't changed out from under Anymail (isn't rejecting Anymail payloads). Travis checks this weekly, and it has caught some changes.
* The other big value of the live tests is debugging the initial implementation. While I'm developing, I add myself as a recipient to the [test_all_options](https://github.com/anymail/django-anymail/blob/master/tests/test_sendgrid_integration.py#L61-L89) live test, and set the environment variables to my own ESP account -- and then carefully inspect the mail I receive and the ESP API logs to make sure everything matches the test message. (And running that test usually uncovers some misunderstanding I had about their API.) Same thing for templates and test_merge_data. Just don't forget to remove yourself before committing.

@looeee that makes it sound like there might be some other issues in your nginx config. (E.g., an nginx 404 trying to load ./html/.../index.html makes it sound like your proxy_pass into Django isn't even being considered on that url. Are other Django pages loading correctly?)

Two suggestions:
* To debug nginx/Django config without Anymail or Mailgun in the way, temporarily create a Django view that just logs and/or returns an HttpResponse with `request.META['HTTP_AUTHORIZATION']`. You should be able to load that with something like https://foo:bar@yoursite.example.com/testview, and the logged auth header should be "Basic *base64-val*" when nginx is properly configured.

See #490 for a fix for this (for movable, non-copyable types).  Because there really is no concept of moving in Python, we really can't actually modify such a vector: when we get a value from Python, we can only copy and reference it, but we can't move it away, so there's basically no way to insert from Python.

That doesn't mean you can't insert at all: just that you have to handle any modification support yourself (i.e. by defining approprimate methods in the class_ object `bind_vector` returns), because any modification support is going to involve `value_type`-specific construction/emplacement.

Re: iterator interface: I don't think it is worthwhile.  The main issue is that the iterator interface has to be added on the *metatype*, not the type itself (because iteration is on `EnumType`, not `EnumType()`).  To support that, we'd have to stop inheriting from `py::class_` and instead add a new metatype for enums (which is what Python itself does).  That's a whole lot of work for very little gain.  Better, I think, to accept this as is, and eventually just use Python's enum metatype (i.e. as proposed in #530) on Python 3.4+, with the current `class_` wrapper providing an incomplete API fallback for < 3.4.

Aside from the `__repr__` simplification I commented on, I think this is good to go.

I messed with this unique_ptr stuff a bit more and came up with a fix for PyBind11. In the template specification for `class_`, we can change `typename holder_type = std::unique_ptr<type>` to the rather long winded but effective
```typename holder_type = std::unique_ptr<type, std::conditional<std::is_destructible<type>::value, std::default_delete<type>, BlankDeleter<type>>::type>```

This would also mean that the BlankDeleter struct I mentioned in my previous post would need to be included. This solves the compile issue for classes with private destructors, or deleted destructors, and in the end I think it means that there's no need to re-implement no_init like boost python does, since, as @wjakob pointed out at the beginning of the thread, you can just not expose the constructor.

A job can be either done or not done. Can’t certbot decide when it got the job done? Laughable.
> b-but muh several states…

For those who didn’t understand: 

- if the certs are not present, they should be created, if they cannot be created – it is a failure; 
- if the certs are expiring and should be renewed – they should be renewed or it is a failure;
- if the certs are not expiring, and there’s no need to renew them – `renew` should return OK, for this state doesn’t harm certificates;
- but if we force the renewal, then renew should update the certs and return OK only on success. And return FAIL if it couldn’t;
- if among the passed domains there are those that expire soon, and those that do not expire, then certbot should return OK if after the end of the procedure there are left no certificates needing renewal.

For the cron job **the only thing that is important, is to know that all of the expiring certificates are updated.**

The nodes must continue with the off-chain protocol, otherwise the lock will stay in the merkle tree forever, keeping the funds locked, even though it should be release allowing the nodes to use the funds with new transfers.

> a node receives a SecretReveal with a reveal timeout not enough to register the secret on-chain -> mediated transfer must fail (correct?)

Another recipe for failure:

1. `sos run workflow -q host`, which generates `.task` files with runtime information geared towards `host`.
2. `sos execute task` would fail because incorrect runtime info. `sos run workflow` would work though because the task would be rewritten with correct runtime info.

Note that `sos execute task -q host` would work, `sos execute task -q host1` would not work in both previous and current version because the runtime information is prepared by `sos run`, not `sos execute`. 

A proper fix would be recording alias of runtime information in the task file so that `sos execute` can properly exit or get the correct runtime information before execution.

I did some more research on the question what the final solution should be:

According to the HTTP 1.1 spec, DELETE is an idempotent method, and idempotency is defined such that a second invocation of the same method does not change the resource state of the system. It may very well behave differently in its HTTP response.

For example, a first DELETE on a resource will change the resource state such that the resource is no longer there, and returns success in its response. A second DELETE on the same resource also leaves the system in a state where that resource does not exist, but returns "not found".

This is pretty much what the HMC implemented for Delete Partition: The second DELETE gets a "busy" error back, because the first DELETE is still in progress. It probably would return "not found" if the retry was performed just when the processing of the first DELETE was complete.

So the HMC behaves according to the HTTP spec for this case.

The `requests` module also behaves according to the HTTP spec when it resends the DELETE request, because DELETE is an idempotent HTTP method.

Nevertheless we do not want to return an error back to the zhmcclient users in such a case.

So the mitigation of turning off read retries may very well end up to be the final solution.

One option still to be investigated is the use of the `method_whitelist` parameter when creating the urllib3 `Retry` object.

> the problem doesn't occur when running from the commandline

Because the streamlink pythonscript is using a different shebang, linking to the python3 executable.

> it seems the error comes from the Streamlink GUI

No, this is not an error. The Twitch GUI is looking up python independently, so if python2 is listed before python3 in the system's PATH env var, it will choose this instead. This is all documented in the wiki and also stated in the settings menu next to the input fields. The user is responsible for making sure that streamlink is working with the *selected* python version.

Another option which may or may not be useful is the VLC command line option `--play-and-pause` , which will pause when it reaches the end of the file. To resume, you have to rewind a few seconds or so before trying to play again, though. Also, occasionally VLC will lock up.

Hi @gwshaw, I think you have raised two issues with a single test:

request-oauthlib
------
A check of HTTP status must be done in `requests-oauthlib` code. For example, we can see `requests-oauthlib` calling directly `oauthlib` `_client`without any check on the `status`. However, `oauthlib` cannot do any check on this because it uses the body only. See https://github.com/requests/requests-oauthlib/issues/302 which is similar. 

oauthlib
-------
Then, `oauthlib` should raise a valid exception when the body is wrong. However, in this usecase, it's not happening because the response is a valid JSON. It's not valid OAuth2.0 response still, but it's correctly parsed by the `json.loads` call because the `"Internal server error"` is surrounded by double-quotes. Not usual, right ?

If you change your `AUTH_TOKEN_URL` from `https://httpstat.us/500`to `https://httpbin.org/status/500`, you will get a valid OAuth exception: 

```python
oauthlib.oauth2.rfc6749.errors.MissingTokenError: (missing_token) Missing access token parameter.
```

A check of the validity of the json (e.g. is it a `dict`?) around https://github.com/oauthlib/oauthlib/blob/master/oauthlib/oauth2/rfc6749/parameters.py#L356-L380 should be added, or when inside the `OAuth2Token` constructor.

Any PRs are welcomed :-)
